1: Elihu speaks directly to Job — a shift from the three friends who addressed each other and the audience. Christ also speaks directly and personally to the individual (John Job 11:23: “Your brother will rise again”; John Job 20:16: “Mary”). The personal address is the mark of genuine mediation.
2: Elihu has opened his mouth; his tongue speaks within his mouth. The self-announcement of the speaker prepares for the greater announcement: the Word made flesh (John Job 1:14), the one whose speech comes not from a human mouth alone but from the Father who sent him (John Job 12:49).
3: Words from an upright heart; lips speak what he knows sincerely. Elihu’s claim of integrity anticipates Christ’s perfect sincerity: “the words that I say to you I do not speak on my own authority, but the Father who dwells in me does his works” (John Job 14:10). Christ’s words are perfectly aligned with his character.
4: “The Spirit of God has made me, and the breath of the Almighty gives me life.” Elihu acknowledges his creaturely dependence on the divine breath — the same nšmāh of Gen Job 2:7. Christ’s resurrection is the new creation: he breathes on his disciples (John Job 20:22), giving the Holy Spirit — the life-giving breath now becomes the Spirit of new creation.
5: If you can answer me, set your words in order before me and take your stand. The call to make a case — and then present Christ as the one whose case stands without defect: 1 Pet Job 3:15 “always be prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you.” Christ is both the advocate and the one whose case cannot be refuted (John Job 8:46: “which one of you convicts me of sin?”).
6: “See, I stand before God on your behalf as you wished; I too was formed from clay.” Elihu’s claim to be a fellow-creature who stands before God on Job’s behalf is the imperfect anticipation of Christ, who is also “made from dust” in the Incarnation (Phil Job 2:7: “born in the likeness of men”) and stands before the Father as humanity’s representative (Heb Job 2:17: “he had to be made like his brothers in every respect... to make propitiation for the sins of the people”).
7: My dread will not terrify you; my pressure will not be heavy upon you. The gentle approach of the true mediator — Christ who says “my yoke is easy and my burden is light” (Matt Job 11:30) and “do not be afraid” (John Job 6:20). Unlike the approach to Sinai (Heb Job 12:18–21), the approach through Christ brings confidence not terror.
8: Elihu reports what Job said — the careful listening that precedes a genuine answer. Christ also hears before he speaks: Heb Job 5:7 “in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence.”
9: “You said: I am clean, without transgression; I am innocent and there is no iniquity in me.” Job’s claim of innocence, which is contested by the friends, is ultimately vindicated by God (Job 42:7). But Christ’s claim of sinlessness is not contested — it is absolute: “In him there is no sin” (1 John Job 3:5); “one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin” (Heb Job 4:15).
10: But God finds grievances against me; regards me as his enemy. The estrangement Job experiences — God as adversary — is what Christ absorbs at the cross. He becomes the enemy of God on behalf of those who were God’s enemies (Rom Job 5:10: “while we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son”).
11: He puts my feet in stocks; he watches all my paths. The prisoner-of-God image. Christ is himself the prisoner at his arrest (John Job 18:12) — the free one bound so that the bound might go free. His paths are watched by the Father because they lead infallibly to redemption.
12: “In this I tell you plainly that you are wrong: God is greater than any human being.” The divine greatness that exceeds human comprehension is the point where Elihu’s theology is correct — and it is the premise of the Incarnation: only one who is greater than any human being could be the mediator that both Job and the cosmos need (1 Tim Job 2:5: “one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus”).
13: Why do you argue against him? He does not give account for any of his actions. The inscrutable freedom of God that exceeds all human questioning — and yet: the cross is God’s self-account, his explanation, his justification of his own ways (Rom Job 3:25–26: “it was to show his righteousness at the present time, so that he might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus”).
14: God speaks in one way and then another, though people do not notice. The various modes of divine revelation enumerated here (dreams, visions, chastening) all anticipate the final speech: Heb Job 1:1–2 “Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.”
15: In a dream, in a nighttime vision, when deep sleep falls on people. God speaks through the unconscious, reaching the person when all defenses are down. The incarnation is the most direct form of this speaking: God entering the full human condition — including the vulnerability of sleep (Matt Job 8:24: Jesus sleeps in the boat; Luke Job 2:7: the infant sleeps).
16: He opens people’s ears and confirms what he is teaching them. The opened ear of the disciple: Isa Job 50:4–5 “morning by morning he wakens my ear to hear as those who are taught” — the Servant of the LORD whose ear is opened is the pattern Christ embodies; and he opens the disciples’ understanding (Luke Job 24:45).
17: To turn them from their intended course and shield them from pride. The purpose of divine discipline: turning and humbling. Christ’s teaching also turns people from the way of destruction (Matt Job 7:13–14) and his cross is the supreme destruction of human pride (1 Cor Job 1:29: “so that no human being might boast in the presence of God”).
18: He keeps him from the pit and his life from crossing into death. God’s preserving purpose in suffering. The ultimate keeping-from-the-pit is the resurrection of Christ: Acts Job 2:24 “God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it” (citing Ps Job 16:10: “you will not abandon my soul to Sheol”).
19: Chastened with pain upon his bed; unending conflict in his bones. Elihu describes the physical suffering of God’s discipline. Heb Job 5:8: “although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered.” Christ himself is the suffering-and-learning servant, though his suffering is not punitive but redemptive.
20: He cannot eat bread; appetite revolts against any food. The fast of suffering. Jesus himself fasted forty days (Matt Job 4:2) and on the cross refused the drugged wine (Mark Job 15:23). He abstained from earthly sustenance to accomplish the work of redemption, offering himself as the true bread (John Job 6:51).
21: His flesh wastes away until it is gone; bones that were hidden now protrude. The bodily dissolution of extreme suffering. The crucified Christ: John Job 19:34 (blood and water from his side); Ps Job 22:14–17 (bones out of joint, strength dried up, feet and hands pierced) — the Passion Psalm describes this exact wasting.
22: His life draws near the pit and his soul to the agents of death. The proximity to death — and then the mediator arrives. Christ descended to the deepest point — Eph Job 4:9 “he had also descended into the lower parts of the earth” — so that his rescue could reach those nearest the pit.
23: “But if there is a messenger for him, a mediator (meliṣ) — one in a thousand — who declares to the person their uprightness.” This is the book’s most explicit mediator-term outside Job 19:25. The meliṣ (interpreter, messenger, mediator) who is one-in-a-thousand finds its fulfilment in Christ: “there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus” (1 Tim Job 2:5). He alone, of all possible advocates, both declares the person’s uprightness and has grounds to do so.
24: “Then God is gracious to him and says: Spare him from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom (kōper).” The ransom-declaration from God is the theological heart of the chapter. Mark Job 10:45: “the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom (lytron) for many.” Heb Job 9:15: Christ “is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance, since a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions committed under the first covenant.” The ransom God finds is his own Son.
25: “His flesh becomes fresher than a child’s; he is restored to the days of his youth.” The restoration through the mediator’s ransom — a new-creation freshness beyond the original state. Tit Job 3:5: “he saved us through the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit.” John Job 3:3: “unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God.” The born-again metaphor is the new-covenant form of the flesh-fresher-than-a-child image Elihu describes.
26: “He prays to God and God accepts him; he sees his face with a shout of joy, and God restores his righteousness to him.” The restoration pattern: prayer, acceptance, sight-of-God, righteousness restored. 1 John Job 3:2: “when he appears we shall be like him, because we shall see him as he is.” The face-to-face vision is the eschatological goal, secured through the mediator’s ransom.
27: He looks on people and says: “I sinned and bent what was right, and it brought me nothing.” The confession that follows rescue rather than preceding it — the person acknowledges sin in the light of grace. 1 John Job 1:9: “if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.” The confession that brings forgiveness is possible only because the ransom has been paid.
28: “God will deliver his life from the pit, and his life will see the light.” The seeing-of-light after rescue from the pit is the resurrection pattern. John Job 1:4–5: “in him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.” The pit cannot hold the one the mediator has ransomed.
29: God does all these things — again and again — with a person. The repeated, patient, redeeming work of God. 2 Pet Job 3:9: “the Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.” The cross is the once-for-all event that grounds the again-and-again patience.
30: To bring his life back from the pit, that he might be lit by the light of the living. The purpose of redemption: not merely rescue from death but lighting-up by life. Christ as “the resurrection and the life” (John Job 11:25) brings those rescued from the pit into a new quality of existence — the light of the living that is nothing less than Christ himself (John Job 8:12).
31: Pay close attention, Job; be silent and I will speak. The invitation to listening silence before the mediator’s word. Heb 1:2 God “has spoken to us by his Son” — the final word that requires the silence of all other speech, the attentive listening of faith.
32: If you have anything to say, answer me; speak up, for I want to see you vindicated. Elihu genuinely desires Job’s vindication — not his condemnation. Christ, the perfect Mediator, desires the vindication of all who come to him: “whoever comes to me I will never cast out” (John Job 6:37); “I did not come to judge the world but to save the world” (John Job 12:47).
33: If not, listen to me; be silent, and I will teach you wisdom. The teaching of wisdom to the silent one. Matt Job 11:29: “take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls.” The wisdom Christ teaches is himself — the one the wisdom poem of ch. 28 was searching for.
